toronto.built: Opened 2007; expanded with temporary seating for the World Cup
toronto.population: Toronto metro ~6.2 million; city ~2.8 million (2021 Census)
toronto.character: Canada's largest city — global, lakeside, and proudly multicultural.
toronto.football_history: Hosted the 2007 FIFA U-20 World Cup final. Hosts Canada's opening match (vs Bosnia, June 12) and five matches total including a Round of 32 tie.
toronto.stadium_fact: BMO Field is the smallest stadium of the 16 host venues, expanded with temporary seating to ~45,700 for the tournament.
toronto.tidbits: Did you know? More than half of Torontonians were born outside Canada — so matchday here feels like a mini World Cup before the whistle. And the CN Tower looms so close to the stadium district it feels like part of the skyline show.
toronto.sources: FIFA; BMO Field official; Statistics Canada 2021 Census

vancouver.character: A Pacific-coast city framed by ocean, forests, and mountains.
vancouver.football_history: Hosted the 2015 FIFA Women's World Cup final. Hosts Canada group-stage matches in 2026.
vancouver.stadium_fact: BC Place is a downtown landmark with a retractable roof — though FIFA requires the roof open during matches for natural light on the grass.
vancouver.tidbits: Did you know? You can watch a match downtown and still see snow-capped mountains from nearby streets. BC Place also hosted the opening and closing ceremonies of the Vancouver 2010 Winter Olympics.
vancouver.sources: FIFA; BC Place official; Statistics Canada 2021 Census

mexico_city.capacity_wc: ~87,500 (post-2026 renovation; ~90,000 max configuration). FIFA tournament capacity listed ~72,800 for seated WC config.
mexico_city.character: Mexico's vast capital — high-altitude, historic, noisy, creative, and football-mad.
mexico_city.clubs: Club América, Cruz Azul, and Pumas UNAM (Liga MX)
mexico_city.football_history: Hosted the 1970 and 1986 World Cup finals. Hosts the 2026 opening match (Mexico vs South Africa, June 11) — becoming the first stadium ever to stage matches in three different men's World Cups.
mexico_city.stadium_fact: One of football's most iconic grounds, recently renovated. At ~2,240 m elevation, it's the highest and oldest venue of the tournament.
mexico_city.tidbits: Did you know? The same pitch saw Pelé lift the 1970 World Cup and Maradona score both the 'Hand of God' and the 'Goal of the Century' in 1986. And at over 2,200 metres above sea level, even the thin air gives the home side an edge.
mexico_city.sources: FIFA; Estadio Banorte official; INEGI 2020 Census; Wikipedia

guadalajara.character: A western Mexican cultural capital — home of mariachi, near tequila country, and fiercely loyal to football.
guadalajara.clubs: Chivas de Guadalajara and Atlas (Liga MX)
guadalajara.football_history: Guadalajara hosted World Cup matches in 1970 and 1986 (at the older Estadio Jalisco). In 2026 it uses Estadio Akron, home of Chivas, for four group matches including Mexico vs Korea Republic.
guadalajara.stadium_fact: Estadio Akron, opened 2010, is known for its cloud-like PTFE roof that lets natural light onto the pitch. It hosted the 2010 Copa Libertadores final and the 2011 Pan American Games ceremonies.
guadalajara.tidbits: Did you know? Chivas are famous for a rare rule: they field Mexican players only. And the city sits beside the very region that gives the world tequila — so Guadalajara serves football heritage and cultural flavour in equal measure.

monterrey.character: A northern industrial powerhouse with mountains on the horizon and fierce club rivalries.
monterrey.clubs: CF Monterrey (Rayados) and Tigres UANL (Liga MX)
monterrey.football_history: Monterrey hosted matches at the 1986 World Cup. In 2026, Estadio BBVA hosts three group matches.
monterrey.stadium_fact: Estadio BBVA, opened 2015, seats ~53,500 and is home to CF Monterrey.
monterrey.tidbits: Did you know? One end of the stadium frames Cerro de la Silla, the saddle-shaped mountain that is Monterrey's postcard view. Few stadium backdrops feel so naturally cinematic.
monterrey.sources: FIFA; Rayados official; Populous; INEGI 2020 Census

atlanta.character: A fast-growing Southern capital known for music, film, civil-rights history, and one of the world's busiest airports.
atlanta.football_history: Atlanta United proved how huge soccer crowds could be in the American South. The city hosts a 2026 semifinal.
atlanta.stadium_fact: Mercedes-Benz Stadium is famous for its retractable 'pinwheel' roof that opens like a camera aperture, and its 360-degree circular halo video board.
atlanta.tidbits: Did you know? Atlanta's airport is regularly ranked the busiest on Earth, so many fans may pass through even if they're heading elsewhere. Inside the stadium, the roof petals open like a giant camera lens.
atlanta.sources: FIFA; Mercedes-Benz Stadium official; US Census MSA estimate

boston.character: A historic New England city-region famous for universities, sports passion, and old-meets-new streets.
boston.football_history: The old Foxboro Stadium hosted 1994 World Cup matches; Gillette Stadium carries the region into 2026, including a quarterfinal.
boston.stadium_fact: Gillette Stadium sits in Foxborough — between Boston and Providence — and is home to the NFL's New England Patriots and the Revolution.
boston.tidbits: Did you know? The venue isn't actually in Boston — it's in Foxborough, a small town where matchday feels like a whole region has driven in together. The Patriots' dynasty was built here.
boston.sources: FIFA; Gillette Stadium official; US Census MSA estimate

dallas.character: A huge North Texas metroplex: business, highways, suburbs, and big-scale sport.
dallas.clubs: FC Dallas (MLS, based in suburban Frisco)
dallas.football_history: Dallas hosted 1994 World Cup matches at the Cotton Bowl. AT&T Stadium hosts NINE matches in 2026 — more than any other venue — including a semifinal.
dallas.stadium_fact: AT&T Stadium, home of the Dallas Cowboys, is the LARGEST venue of the 2026 World Cup at ~94,000, with a retractable roof and a famous giant centre-hung video board.
dallas.tidbits: Did you know? Locals nicknamed it 'Jerry World' after Cowboys owner Jerry Jones. Its centre-hung video board is so vast it became part of the building's identity — in Texas, even the screen is supersized. It hosts more matches than any other 2026 venue.
dallas.sources: FIFA; AT&T Stadium official; US Census MSA estimate

houston.character: A Gulf Coast mega-city known for energy, space science, immigration, and food from everywhere.
houston.clubs: Houston Dynamo FC (MLS) and Houston Dash (NWSL)
houston.football_history: Houston regularly hosts Gold Cup and Copa América matches and is a steady U.S. soccer destination.
houston.stadium_fact: NRG Stadium, opened 2002, has a retractable roof and is home to the NFL's Houston Texans.
houston.tidbits: Did you know? 'Houston' is one of the most famous words ever spoken in space history ('Houston, we have a problem'). Back on Earth, its food scene is as global as the tournament — Vietnamese, Mexican, Nigerian, Indian, and Texas barbecue all thrive here.
houston.sources: FIFA; NRG Park official; US Census MSA estimate

kansas_city.built: Opened 1972 — the oldest U.S. venue hosting 2026 matches
kansas_city.character: A two-state metro known for barbecue, jazz roots, fountains, and devoted sports crowds.
kansas_city.football_history: Sporting KC are an established MLS club, and the city hosts six 2026 matches — including Messi's Argentina vs Algeria group game.
kansas_city.stadium_fact: Arrowhead, opened 1972, is the oldest U.S. venue at the tournament, home to the NFL's Chiefs, and famous for record-setting crowd noise (~142.2 decibels, a Guinness record).
kansas_city.tidbits: Did you know? Kansas City straddles a state line — half is in Missouri, half in Kansas. And Arrowhead set a Guinness-recognized record as the loudest stadium on Earth, so this may be one of the noisiest stops of the tournament.
kansas_city.sources: FIFA; Chiefs official; Sporting KC; US Census MSA estimate

los_angeles.capacity_wc: ~70,000 (WC configuration; expandable to 100,000+ for major events)
los_angeles.character: A global entertainment capital — film, music, beaches, immigrant neighbourhoods, and legendary traffic.
los_angeles.clubs: LA Galaxy and Los Angeles FC (MLS)
los_angeles.football_history: The 1994 World Cup final was played at the Rose Bowl in the LA region; 2026 shifts to SoFi Stadium, where the USA opens its campaign.
los_angeles.stadium_fact: SoFi Stadium, opened 2020, is famous for its translucent roof canopy and a giant dual-sided 'Oculus' video board hanging over the field. It hosts Super Bowl LX in Feb 2026.
los_angeles.tidbits: Did you know? SoFi is in Inglewood, not central LA — but that's very LA: the city is really a constellation of places. Hollywood glamour, beach culture, and elite sport all collide here.
los_angeles.sources: FIFA; SoFi Stadium official; US Census MSA estimate

miami.character: A warm, multilingual gateway between North America, the Caribbean, and Latin America.
miami.football_history: Miami hosts major friendlies and Copa América matches, and became a global MLS spotlight after Messi joined Inter Miami. Hard Rock hosts the 2026 third-place (bronze) final.
miami.stadium_fact: Hard Rock Stadium hosts the NFL's Dolphins, the Miami Open tennis tournament, and the Formula One Miami Grand Prix.
miami.tidbits: Did you know? The stadium is in Miami Gardens, north of Miami proper. Few World Cup cities will feel more Latin American on the street — Spanish, Caribbean rhythms, and football talk are everywhere.
miami.sources: FIFA; Hard Rock Stadium official; Inter Miami; US Census MSA estimate

new_york_new_jersey.character: North America's biggest metro — skyscrapers, global finance, culture, media, and nonstop energy.
new_york_new_jersey.clubs: New York City FC and New York Red Bulls (MLS)
new_york_new_jersey.football_history: The region hosted 1994 World Cup matches at Giants Stadium. MetLife hosts the 2026 FINAL on July 19 (and hosted the 2025 FIFA Club World Cup final).
new_york_new_jersey.stadium_fact: MetLife Stadium seats ~82,500 and is home to BOTH the NFL's Giants and Jets — the only stadium shared by two NFL teams.
new_york_new_jersey.tidbits: Did you know? The final is branded 'New York/New Jersey,' but the stadium is physically in East Rutherford, New Jersey — a detail that starts friendly debates before the first ball is kicked.
new_york_new_jersey.sources: FIFA; MetLife Stadium official; Al Jazeera venue guide; US Census MSA estimate

philadelphia.character: A historic American city with passionate fans, neighbourhood pride, murals, and famous cheesesteaks.
philadelphia.football_history: Philadelphia hosts six 2026 matches including a Round of 16 tie, and has a strong modern MLS presence through the Union.
philadelphia.stadium_fact: Lincoln Financial Field — 'The Linc' — opened 2003 and is home to the NFL's Philadelphia Eagles.
philadelphia.tidbits: Did you know? Philadelphia is where the United States signed its Declaration of Independence — so you can go from football chants to the Liberty Bell in one trip. And everyone will insist their cheesesteak spot is the only correct one.
philadelphia.sources: FIFA; Lincoln Financial Field official; Philadelphia Union; US Census MSA estimate

san_francisco_bay_area.population: SF Bay Area ~9 million; SF-Oakland metro ~4.6 million (2025 estimate)
san_francisco_bay_area.character: A high-tech, high-cost, high-scenery region where Silicon Valley meets the Pacific.
san_francisco_bay_area.football_history: The Bay Area hosted 1994 World Cup matches at Stanford Stadium; 2026 uses Levi's Stadium in Santa Clara, which also hosted Super Bowl LX in Feb 2026.
san_francisco_bay_area.stadium_fact: Levi's Stadium, opened 2014, is home to the NFL's San Francisco 49ers — though it sits in Santa Clara, deep in Silicon Valley. It holds LEED Gold certification with solar panels and a green roof.
san_francisco_bay_area.tidbits: Did you know? The 'San Francisco' venue is closer to Silicon Valley tech campuses than to the Golden Gate Bridge. Fans may arrive expecting fog and cable cars, then find themselves in the land of chips, code, and sunshine.
san_francisco_bay_area.sources: FIFA; Levi's Stadium official; San Jose Earthquakes; US Census estimate

seattle.character: A Pacific Northwest city known for coffee, tech, music, rain jokes, and water-and-mountain views.
seattle.football_history: The Sounders are one of MLS's strongest fan-culture stories and won the CONCACAF Champions League in 2022. Lumen hosts six 2026 matches, including USA vs Australia and a Round of 16.
seattle.stadium_fact: Lumen Field, home to the Sounders and NFL's Seahawks, has a steep horseshoe bowl and roof design built to amplify crowd noise.
seattle.tidbits: Did you know? Seattle fans helped make MLS feel big-league in the U.S., regularly turning league matches into stadium-sized events. And yes — this is the hometown of Starbucks, so the coffee reputation is real.
seattle.sources: FIFA; Lumen Field official; Seattle Sounders; US Census MSA estimate
